title: Learning From Disagreement in the U.S. Treasury Bond Market
abstract: We study risk premiums in the U.S. Treasury bond market from the perspective of a Bayesian econometricianBLwho learns in real time from disagreement among investors about future bond yields. Notably, disagreement has substantial predictive power for yields, andBL's risk premiums are less volatile than those in the analogous model without learning.BL's forecasts are substantially more accurate than the consensus forecasts of market professionals, particularly following U.S. recessions. The predictive power of disagreement is distinct from the (much weaker) one of inflation and output growth. Rather, it appears to reflect uncertainty about future fiscal policy.

article.introduction:
Most of the literature that studiesrisk compensation in bond markets follows Dai and Singleton (2000) and Duffee (2002) in assuming that investors have full knowledge of the dynamics of risk factors in bond markets. However, market participants trading U.S. Treasury bonds need to form prospective views in real-time on expected returns, while assessing the implications of changing policy, regulatory, and political environments for yields. That real-time inference can materially impact measured risk premiums is illustrated in Figure1.1For a one-quarter holding period on a 10-year Treasury zero-coupon bond, the within-sample, least-squares forecasts of (annualized) excess returns differ from the forecasts from a real-time, recursive least-squares (RLS) learning model by as much as 4%; analogous differences for the one-year horizon exceed 5%.2Moreover, the mechanical end-of-sample convergence of the recursive to the full-sample least-squares estimates is quite slow.
In this paper, we explore in depth the structure and complexity of ex ante risk premiums in U.S. Treasury markets through the lens of a Bayesian learner,BL, who updates her views about the parameters governing the evolution of bond yields within a dynamic term structure model (DTSM). A novel feature of our analysis is thatBLconditions her learning on the history of disagreement among professional forecasters about future bond yields. Including disagreement significantly increases the accuracy ofBL's predictions of bond yields (and impacts measured risk premiums), relative to the benchmark case of learning from yield information alone.
There are compelling theoretical motivations for equilibrium effects of heterogeneous beliefs on bond yields (see Xiong and Yan (2009), Buraschi and Whelan (2016), Ehling et al. (2018)). Most empirical research focuses on disagreement about macroeconomic fundamentals (e.g., Andrade et al. (2016), Buraschi and Whelan (2016)), consistent with the evidence that these fundamentals affect risk compensation in the bond market (e.g., Ludvigson and Ng (2010), Joslin, Priebsch, and Singleton (2014) (JPS)). We show that output growth and inflation, as well as disagreement about these fundamentals, are largely redundant as predictors of risk premiums once we condition on disagreement about future yields. Instead, our findings suggest a connection between risk premiums on long-term bonds, yield disagreement, and uncertainty about future government spending (fiscal policy). These findings shed new light on the channels through which disagreement drives prices and risk premiums in bond markets.
While Figure1provides compelling evidence that learning can shape beliefs about expected excess returns, we gain deeper insights intoBL's learning problem by framing it within a DTSM. In particular, the regression approach underlying Figure1is uninformative about how real-time learning affects the market prices of risks (MPRs), nor does it reveal the parameters of the pricing distribution. Moreover, forecast accuracy may increase within a DTSM (relative to unconstrained regressions) whenBL's prior knowledge of the pricing distribution disciplines her beliefs through constraints on the MPRs (Joslin, Singleton, and Zhu (2011) (JSZ)). Such discipline potentially arises in our setting because of the constraintsBLimposes on MPRs when training her learning rule.3
To benchmark our core analysis, we first study the case in which risk compensation is determined only by the risk factors, that is, variation inBL's expected excess returns is driven entirely by learning about the dynamics of the low-order yield principal components (PCs). We find that parameter updating is a quantitatively important source of variation in risk premiums, and that the fitted MPRs fromBL's learning rule arelessvolatile than those implied by its full-sample/no-learning counterpart. This suggests that prior studies of DTSMs that abstract from learning may have overstated the time variation in investors' MPRs.
Furthermore, we find thatBL's forecasts—even when not conditioned on disagreement—are substantially more accurate than the consensus Blue Chip Financial Forecasts (BCFF) of yields for both one- and four-quarter-ahead horizons. This is especially true when the U.S. economy is emerging from recessions.4Including yield disagreement inBL's information set further improves her forecast accuracy for one-year-ahead excess returns on 10-year bonds by over 10%. This increased precision comes from two channels: (i) the MPRs of the factor risks depend directly on measured disagreement, and (ii) disagreement informs the real-time updating of parameters governing the DTSM's temporal variation in the risk factors.
The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIprovides further theoretical motivation for our analysis and introduces our measures of disagreement about bond yields. In SectionII, we argue that market participants are reasonably viewed as being quite knowledgeable about the pricing distribution for bonds, and we build on this observation in developing our formal model of learning about the historical distribution of yields. Parameter estimates and forecasts of yields and risk premia from our learning models are discussed in SectionsIIIandIV. In SectionV, we compare the predictive power of yield disagreement to that of various macroeconomic variables, and we also document an intriguing link between yields disagreement, uncertainty about fiscal policy, and excess returns on Treasury bonds. Finally, in SectionVIwe conclude.
There is substantial disagreement among market professionals about future paths of bond yields. To quantify yield disagreement, we study U.S. Treasury zero-coupon bond yields with maturities of six months and 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, and 10 years. For a matching set of bond maturities, we construct empirical counterparts to disagreement using the BCFF survey of yield forecasts over the period January 1985 through December 2015, with the start date determined by data availability. This monthly survey is typically released at the beginning of the following month (usually the first business day), based on information collected over a two-day period (typically scheduled between the 20thand the 26thof the month). Both the zero yields and their corresponding survey-implied forecasts are computed as in Le and Singleton (2012). Disagreement is measured as the difference between the 90thand 10thpercentiles of the cross-sectional distribution of BCFF forecasts. We denote disagreement over horizonjfor bond maturitymin monthtasIDjt(ym).5
Figure2illustrates the historical patterns in disagreement for the 2- and 10-year points on the yield curve. We find thatID1y(y2y)tends to exceedID1y(y10y), with the gap between them, (ID1y(y2y)−ID1y(y10y)), being relatively large following recessions. The years 2012 and 2013 are exceptional for the persistently low level ofID1y(y2y), as well as for the low level ofIDjt(y2y)over predictive horizons (j) of one, two, and three quarters.
A widely studied theoretical mechanism through which disagreement affects equilibrium asset prices has market participants “agreeing to disagree” about the future evolution of prices and then speculating against each other based on their differences in views.6In such a setting, Xiong and Yan (2009) show that speculative motives induce trading and fluctuations in relative wealths of log-utility investors that, in turn, affect equilibrium prices and risk premiums in bond markets. Buraschi and Whelan (2016) and Ehling et al. (2018) extend the Xiong-Yan model to constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) preferences and preference shocks, and explore the effects of disagreement about output growth and inflation on bond prices. An interesting exception is Cao et al. (2017), who provide an equilibrium interpretation of our finding that yield disagreement has a significant impact onBL's expected excess returns.
In this paper, we step back from this differences-of-opinion framework to address the broader quantitative question of how disagreement impacts learning about risk premiums in U.S. Treasury markets. Our learning framework is disciplined more by known features of market participants' pricing rules than by the parametric economic structures of prior two-agent equilibrium models. As an “outside observer” of bond markets,BLapproaches her learning problem with several insights in hand: (i) the covariance structure of bond yields is reliably represented by a low-dimensional factor model with the yields low-order PCs as factors (Litterman and Scheinkman (1991)), (ii) the cross-sectional covariance structure of yields in an arbitrage-free market essentially reveals the PC loadings to investors; and (iii) MPRs vary over the business cycle. These insights are easily accommodated within a reduced-form DTSM. Achieving (i) within a difference-of-opinion model typically requires that only a small number of belief classes are relevant for pricing in bond markets. In addition, constraints onBL's beliefs about excess returns that arise from a priori knowledge connecting (ii) and (iii) are easily enforced within a DTSM; whereas they are typically not explored (or enforced) in equilibrium models.
Professional traders typically adopt a pricing model that shares the factor structure of (3). This industry practice presumes knowledge of the loadings(Am,Bm), asΘQis treated as both known and fixed over the interval[t,t+m]when computingDtm. By following this practice,BLcan precisely calibrateλQfrom the loadingsBm(λQ)and the cross-maturity factor structure ofytmandPt(see also Duffee (2011)). The interceptsAmalso depend onk∞QandΣPP. However, the impact ofΣPPonAmis through a convexity adjustment that is typically very small. Therefore, knowledge ofλQand a tight prior onk∞Q(also estimable from the cross section of yields) imply thatBLeffectively knows theAms as well.
Taken together, these observations suggest that (i)BL's central learning problem in Treasury markets pertains to the physical dynamics of the risk factors rather than the risk-neutral (pricing) distribution of yields, and (ii) investors' a priori knowledge of aspects of the pricing distribution may serve to discipline the real-time learning rules underlying their subjective expected excess returns. We pursue these possibilities more formally in SectionII.
In Panel A of TableI, we report theR2s from projections of measured disagreement onto bond yields for forecast horizons of one through four quarters. Yield disagreement is only partially spanned by the yield curve, withR2s ranging from 40% to 69% across forecast horizons and bond maturities.
At the same time, theIDjt(ym)are mutually highly correlated. The first PC of the covariance matrix ofIDjt(ym)explains 97%, and the first two PCs explain more than 99%, of the variation in disagreement. Accordingly, henceforth we summarize the information on bond yield disagreement at timetbyHt′=[IDt(y2y),IDt(y10y)]over the horizon of one year (so we drop the subscriptj=1y). The first PC is a normalized average of disagreementID(ym)across yields, while the second PC has a correlation with(ID(y10y)−ID(y2y))of 0.82, so in effectHcaptures the “level” and “slope” of disagreement.
Buraschi and Whelan (2016) and Andrade et al. (2016), among others, present evidence that disagreement about future output growth and inflation have predictive power for yields. We focus on yield disagreement because the priced factors in bond markets are spanned by the low-dimensional yield PCs. Moreover, yield disagreement is not spanned by disagreement about inflation and output growth, again measured as inter decile ranges for the sameBCFFforecasters.8Panel B of TableIshows thatR2s increase only marginally when theID(ym)are projected onto the yield PCs together withID(CPI)andID(RGDP), and these variables account only for 14% of the variation inID(y10y)−ID(y2y).
TableIIreports regressions for full-sample predictions of realized excess returns over a one-quarter horizon based on various combinations of the conditioning variablesPt′,IDt(y2y), andIDt(y10y). The coefficients onID(y2y)andID(y10y)are all statistically significant, have opposite signs, and are comparable in absolute magnitudes (the slope of disagreement matters).9Similar patterns obtain for excess returns over a one-year horizon, though adjustedR2s more than double. We explore this evidence further in the context of our learning model in SectionIV, and we investigate the economic underpinnings of the role ofHtin learning in SectionV.
article.II. FormalizingBL's Learning Rule:
When learning about the historical dynamics of the risk factorsPt,BLdraws up the broader informationZ1t−1≡{Z1,Z2,…,Zt−1}that subsumesP1t−1. One motivation for this is that the market prices of the factor risks will generally depend on more information than bond yields alone (see JPS for discussion of this point in a nonlearning setting). In addition, this allowsBLto update her posterior means of the unknown parameters offP(Pt|Z1t−1)with the arrival of richer information thanP1t−1alone. Given our focus on disagreement inBL's learning rule, in most of our analysis we setZt′=(Pt′,Ht′). However, to shed broader light on the nature of the incremental forecasting power ofHt, we subsequently expandZtto include various macroeconomic variables that potentially have predictive power for future bond returns.
We subsequently focus on the special case in whichPt−1−1Qt−2=(1−δ)·I,11for a constant scalar0<δ≤1, under whichψ̂tbecomes the constant-gain least squares (CGLS) estimator ofψPwithγ=δ. By giving less weight to values ofZfar in the past,BLis adaptively responding to changes in the structure of the economy as captured by (8), with the gain coefficientγdetermining the “half-life” of the weight on past data. As shown in AppendixA, conditional onΘQ, the first-order conditions of the likelihood function implied by Bayesian learning withCGLSupdating are identical to those of a likelihood with terms of the formγtεZtP′ΣZ−1εZtP.
It is instructive to compareBL's updating rule forψ̂tPto the Bayesian learning rule about an unknown, constantψ. This special case arises by settingγ=1, in which caseQt=0in (8) and the updating ofψ̂tPsimplifies to RLS. For this special case, the variance of the posterior distribution forψtP(Pt) is shrinking to zero as sample size grows (see the special case of (A5) in AppendixAwithQt=0). Consequently, sharp changes in market conditions as captured byZmay have negligible effects onψ̂tP: an RLS-basedBLbecomes virtually nonadaptive onψ̂P. Moreover, learning aboutψPcan be implausibly fast.
We believe that time-varying parameters better capture the challenges faced by market participants in forecasting yield dynamics. They give rise to confounding among unknown parameters and states (unpredictable shifts over time inψtP) inBL's learning process, so her posterior variance does not shrink toward zero as her data history expands. A similar outcome could have been achieved with fixedψPby introducing joint learning about fixed parameters and unobserved fundamentals (see, for example, Johannes, Lochstoer, and Mou (2016)). Indeed, filtering on unobserved components of output is a central feature of differences-of-opinion models of bond prices. WithinBL's DTSM-based learning framework, in contrast, the risk factors are the readily observable yield PCs, and thus there is no filtering problem forP.
Moreover,BL's learning problem embeds the feature of “shifting endpoints” introduced by Kozicki and Tinsley (2001) and adopted subsequently in many studies. Bond yields embody a stochastic trend owing toK0tPin (5) following a random walk. Consequently, long-horizon forecasts of yields may change stochastically over time (rather than converge to constants as in stationary models), and this often improves out-of-sample forecasts from affine term structure models (van Dijk et al. (2014) and Bauer and Rudebusch (2020)). In our setting,BLlearns aboutK0tPin real-time conditional on the history ofZt, whereasK0tPis often presumed to be known and well proxied by observable economic trend variables.
The constraintsϕron(K0tP,KZtP)in (5) mitigate overfitting of the forecasts of yields relative to unconstrained linear regressions. As we explain in SectionIII,BLselects constraints during a model “training period” that predates her learning. As such, she is truly computing out-of-sample forecasts using a real-time learning rule.
Throughout this construction, the direct dependence ofΩtonΣZis a consequence ofBLtreatingΣZas known, and not as an object to be learned. This is an admittedly strong assumption as, empirically,BL's learning rule shows revisions inΣZ. Though revisions inΣPPthrough learning would be largely inconsequential for pricing (convexity effects are small), they could be material for how she updates her beliefs aboutΘP. In AppendicesFandG, we show that our core findings are robust to the introduction of learning about the structure of the conditional covariances ofZin a model with time-varying second moments.
article.III. Learning from the Yield Curve: Rule:
Equipped with the parametric learning rule used byBL, we next explore empirically the properties of risk premiums and the role of disagreement in shaping the econometrician's views about future excess returns. As a benchmark, it is instructive to focus initially on learning from information in the yield curve alone. This also allows for learning over a much longer time period (owing to the relatively shorter time series available for BCFF data).
Consider a three-factor DTSM, in whichBLfollows a constant-gain learning rule with conditioning on past information onPalone. This first rule is trained over the period from June 1961 through January 1972. Every month thereafter through December 2014,BLupdates her posterior views on parameters as new data become available. Since this is the longest sample we consider in our study, we denote this rule byℓCGL(P).
To reduce the dimension of the parameter space during the training period,BLsets constraints on the scaled MPRsΛPtin (14), that is, on the difference between thePandQdrifts ofPt. The unrestricted learning model is estimated first, and then any of the differences in coefficients(K0tP−K0Q)and(KPP,tP−KPPQ)withp-values above 0.4 (computed using the posterior variance at the end of the training sample) are set to zero: aPcoefficient is set equal to itsQcounterpart.13Such constrained entries of thePdrift forPtare denoted byQin TableIII; the “*” entries are unconstrained. The last row of TableIIIimplies thatP3t(the third PC) is an unpriced risk inBL's learning rule. This is consistent with prior literature on affine models for U.S. Treasury yields without learning (see, e.g., table II in JPS).
The gain parameterγis set to 0.99. AppendixCoffers two complementary perspectives on this choice. First, if we allowBLto adjustγover time based on realized out-of-sample forecast accuracy for both one- and four-quarter-ahead horizons, she selects fittedγt's close to 0.99. This is especially true over the period January 1995 through December 2014, which is the focus of most of our subsequent analysis. Second, searching over fixedγ's to minimize out-of-sample forecast errors at the one-quarter- and one-year-ahead horizons leads to an “optimal” value ofγthat is approximately 0.99.
Figure4displays the real-time estimates ofλQfrom ruleℓCGL(P). Notably,BLholdsλQvirtually fixed over the entire sample, consistent with the premise of her Bayesian rule for learning aboutΘtP. Though there is mild drift in the second eigenvalueλ2Q, repeating our learning exercise with the full vectorλQfixed from the initial training period onward gives quantitatively similar forecasts.14This is reflected in the loadingsB(λQ)(see equation (3)) of the first two PCs, as they are close to constant over the entire sample. Thus,BLuses nearly fixed “hedge ratios” in managing bond portfolio risks.
This finding is especially striking in relation to howBLupdates the historical eigenvaluesλP(see Figure4). Her views about the objective feedback matrixKPP,tPchange substantially over time, and thus the implied responses of risk premiums to innovations inPchange as well. Yet even during the turbulent period of the Fed experiment in the early 1980s, when there were large changes inλtP,λQremained remarkably stable.
The contrasting behaviors of thePandQeigenvalues suggest that real-time learning may generate different MPRs than the full-sample, fixed-parameter estimates. This is indeed the case, with the MPRs for bothP1andP2from the full-sample estimates beingmorevolatile than their counterparts from the learning ruleℓCGL(P)(Figure5). ForP1, the full-sample MPRs have deeper troughs and higher peaks. In the case of the slope factorP2, the gaps are large and persistent, especially following the NBER recessions. (The very large spike downward inBL'sP2MPR occurred in October 2008, following a major coordinated intervention by central banks and the bail-out of U.S. money market funds.) Thus, for our sample period and relative toBL's learning rule, full-sample estimates overstate the cyclical volatility of risk premiums in the Treasury market. This result suggests that learning dampens the variation in risk premiums required to rationalize the failure of the expectations theory, relative to the full-sample analysis in Dai and Singleton (2002).
Finally, it is instructive to explore the implications of shifting endpoints inBL's learning rule for the implicit shifting “long-run means” of the bond yields. If the risk factorsPtfollowed a stationary stochastic process, then the steady-state mean ofPtwould be(I−KZP)−1K0P. In the process of learning,BLis updating the parameters in the state process (5) every month. We can then calculate a time series of pseudo long-run meansE[P]t≡(I−KZtP)−1K0tP. The linear combinationsAt10y+Bt10yE[P]t(as in (3)) give the temporal decomposition of the pseudo long-run means of the 10-year yield displayed in Figure6.15
The dominant shifting endpoint is that of the level factorPC1, and it follows a trend-like pattern that is qualitatively similar to the stochastic trend in inflation that has often been the focal point of this literature (Kozicki and Tinsley (2001) and Cieslak and Povala (2015)). The pseudo-mean of the slope factorPC2exhibits an intuitively plausible cyclical pattern.16It plays a relatively minor role up to the global financial crisis, after which shifts in both the level and the slope factors contribute equally to the drift in the long-run mean ofy10y.
article.IV. Learning from Disagreement: RuleℓCG(:
We turn next to the central question of the incremental role of disagreement inBL's learning conditioned onZt′=(Pt′,Ht′), ruleℓCG(P,H). In assessing the impact ofHon the risk premiums, we benchmark this rule against two alternatives (besides ruleℓCG(P), which conditions on the PCs alone). The first,ℓ(BCFF), is the forecast rule implicitly used by the median professional forecaster as surveyed by the BCFF. The second is the simple yield-based rule that has each zero yield following a random walk, ruleℓ(RW). BCFF forecasts are averages over calendar quarters and cover horizons out to five quarters ahead. For example, in January 1999, the one-quarter-ahead forecast of a yield is the average of its forecasted values for February, March, and April. For comparability across all forecast rules, we compute similar quarterly averages for rulesℓCG(P),ℓCG(P,H), andℓ(RW).
RuleℓCG(P,H)is trained (and ruleℓCG(P)is retrained for comparability) from January 1985 through January 1995, since data forHtare available only from January 1985. The parsimony imposed on the autoregressiveP-feedback matrix for ruleℓCG(P,H)is displayed in TableIV. DisagreementHis a priced risk in the Treasury bond market through its (strong) correlation withP; it does not directly enter theQdynamics ofP. Therefore, a zero constraint inKZtPon anID(y)literally sets the parameter governing thePdynamics of thisID(y)to zero. As before, constraints on thePautoregression forPtake the form of equating aPparameter to itsQcounterpart. For instance, in thePautoregression forP2(second row of TableIV), the coefficient on laggedP1is constrained to its value under theQdistribution ofP, and laggedHhas no impact. Overall, these constraints show that the primary dynamic feedback betweenID(y10y)andPis throughP1, whereasID(y2y)is influenced by the entireZt−1. After training her rule,BLlearns over time using a monthly expanding window of(P,H).
The relative accuracies of yields forecasts can be assessed from the root mean-squared errors (RMSEs) displayed in TableV. Below each RMSE are Diebold and Mariano (1995) (D-M) statistics for assessing whether two RMSEs are statistically the same. To address the possibility that the standard D-M test has the wrong size in finite samples, we use the small-sample adjustment of Harvey, Leybourne, and Newbold (1997)17and base inference on theT-Studentquantiles with degrees of freedom equal to the forecast sample size minus one. For the full sample from January 1995 through December 2014, the relevant quantiles are 1.65 (1.97) for the 10% (5%) two-sided hypothesis tests.
RuleℓCG(P,H)leads to more accurate forecasts than ruleℓ(RW)across the maturity spectrum (most significantly for long-maturity bonds). Equally notable is the outperformance ofℓCG(P,H)relative to the least accurate ruleℓ(BCFF); the difference is statistically significant across the entire yield curve for the one-quarter horizon.18
Learning from disagreement is a key factor in driving outperformance. For bonds with maturities under five years, the adjusted D-M statistics for comparingℓCG(P,H)againstℓCG(P)show no statistical difference in forecast performance at standard confidence levels. However, for longer maturity bonds the D-M statistics for the four-quarter- (one-quarter-) ahead horizon is −1.63 (−1.89) for five-year bonds, −2.04 (−2.21) for seven-year bonds, and −2.51 (−2.94) for 10-year bonds. In AppendixD, we construct bootstrapped D-Mt-statistics and find that they are similar to those reported in TableV, which reassures us that the statistical outperformance ofℓCG(P,H)is not an artifact of small-sample bias.
Conditioning onHinℓCG(P,H)substantially increases the volatility of the implied MPRs relative to those fromℓCG(P). Over our full sample, the standard deviation of the estimated MPR for the first (second) PC is 0.69% (1.24%) for ruleℓCG(P), while it is almost 2.5% (3.0%) for ruleℓCG(P,H). With the latter rule's higher volatility in risk premiums comes greater forecast accuracy overℓCG(P), particularly for long-term bonds during and immediately after the recessions of 2001 and 2008.
Both learning rules in (16) and (17) outperformℓ(BCFF), especially following NBER recessions. Key to understanding this finding is the positive correlation betweenBL's risk premiums and the steepness of the yield curve (see Figure8for the case of 10-year bonds). When the Treasury yield curve is steep the median BCFF forecaster believes that risk compensation is much lower than whatBLbelieves. For example, following the low (recession) levels ofy10yfrom late 2002 until 2004, the BCFF forecasters expected a much more rapid rise iny10ythan didBL's more accurate rules. The often-heard advice to reduce bond positions as the U.S. economy emerges from recessions, while consistent with the beliefs of the median BCFF forecaster, has in fact been poor advice relative to the ex ante signals fromBL's rules and, with the benefit of hindsight, relative to the actual performance of bonds.19
TableVIreports out-of-sample RMSEs for one-year-ahead excess returns leading up to and after the global financial crisis. Forecasting Treasury yields was relatively easy during the crisis and postcrisis subperiod. With short-term rates pegged near zero,ℓ(RW)performed very well. Notably, the nonlinear updating inherent inBL's learning ruleℓCG(P,H)was quite effective at adjusting for the effects of the Federal Reserve's policy of near-zero short rates on term premiums, without having enforced a zero lower bound onrt(as, for example, in Kim and Singleton (2012) and Christensen and Rudebusch (2015)). During the midst of the crisis, from 2008 to 2011,ℓCG(P,H)outperformedℓ(RW)across all maturity points.
There are sizable differences, however, betweenexr1y,t10y(P,H)andexr1y,t10y(P,0), that is, there are large direct effects ofHonexr1y,t10y, especially around turning points (left panel of Figure9). Comparison of the last two rows of each panel in TableVIshows that these direct effects were quantitatively important forBL's forecast accuracy on long-maturity bonds up to the beginning of the global crisis. The indirect effects of learning through the loadings onP(right panel of Figure9) were largest around turning points (including after recessions) up to about 2012. They are then virtually nonexistent afterward.
article.V. Macro Risks and Disagreement about Yi:
The evidence on the performance of modelℓCG(P,H)described in the previous section leads us to explore how the predictive power ofHrelates to macroeconomic conditions. Motivated by the evidence in Ludvigson and Ng (2010) and JPS, the first issue we address is whetherHinBL's learning ruleℓCG(P,H)serves as a proxy for macroeconomic fundamentals. We then explore connections between disagreement about bond yields, economic policy uncertainty, and bond risk premiums. To formally evaluate the contributions of macro variables toBL's learning, we again use the dynamic learning framework of SectionII, setting the gain coefficient toγ=0.99, and setting the conditioning informationZtoP(the yields PCs) and the additional macro variables of interest.
Using the BCFF panel of forecasters, we construct consensus (median) forecasts of one-year CPI inflationCons(CPI)and real GDP growthCons(RGDP)from the monthly cross sections of forecasters.22Disagreement about one-year-ahead inflationID(CPI)and growthID(RGDP)are measured each month as the inter decile ranges across one-year-ahead forecasts in the BCFF panel. Disagreement about the macroeconomy increases during times of weak economic conditions, as inflation and real economic activity decline.
TableVIIshows that ruleℓCG(P,REA,INF)performance in forecasting excess returns is indistinguishable from that of ruleℓCG(P). Indeed, the RMSEs from the macro learning rule are comparable to those fromℓCG(P)on bonds of maturities up to five years, and larger for longer maturities. Moreover,ℓCG(P,REA,INF)underperforms againstBL's ruleℓCG(P,H)by statistically significant amounts for all longer-term bonds: the D-M statistics for the (5y, 7y, 10y) bonds are (2.49, 2.53, 2.36).23The outperformance ofℓCG(P,H)relative toℓCG(P,REA,INF), shown in Figure10for expected excess returns on 10-year bonds, is greatest during and immediately after NBER recessions. Including information about the beliefs of professionals on the future evolution of macroeconomic variables(Const(RGDP),Const(CPI))or(IDt(RGDP),IDt(CPI))leads to sizabledeteriorationsin forecast accuracy relative to ruleℓCG(P),across the entire maturity spectrum.
CouldHbe proxying for macroeconomic uncertainty or “market fear”? Two measures of such sentiment are the MOVE Index of implied volatility in U.S. Treasury markets,IVol, and the measure of monetary policy uncertainty,MPU, constructed by Backer, Bloom, and Davis (2016).24As with the preceding macro variables, the forecast accuracies of rulesℓCG(P,IVol,MPU)and the benchmarkℓCG(P)are statistically indistinguishable.
We emphasize that all of the macro risks examined above may be priced risks in U.S. Treasury bond markets. Our findings indicate that (i) any relevant information for predicting excess returns spanned by this menu of variables is encompassed byPin our benchmark learning ruleℓCG(P), and (ii)Hhas distinct forecast power for excess returns relative to both these macro variables andP.
Why is disagreement about future bond yields incrementally informative about the risk profiles of long-term bonds? The near cointegration between inflation and long-bond yields points to inflation (or possibly disagreement about inflation) as one candidate explanation for this finding. However, Duffee (2018) and Gomez-Cram and Yaron (2020) find that neither variation in expected inflation nor the volatility of inflation are major sources of variation in yields or risk premiums during most of our sample period (which postdates the Volcker disinflation). Cyclical variation in real economic activity, at least as captured byREAorCons(RGDP), appears to be too high frequency to be relevant for long-term bonds or to have effects that are spanned byP, as neither of these measures improveBL's forecast accuracy relative toℓCG(P).
In this section, we explore another natural explanation, namely, the connection between bond risk premiums, yield disagreement, and fiscal policy. There is mixed evidence on the effects of shocks to government debt to GDP ratios on long-bond yields (e.g., Dai and Philippon (2006), Evans and Marshall (2007)). Building on the work of Pastor and Veronesi (2013) in settings without learning, Leippold and Matthys (2017) and Kucera, Kocenda, and Marsal (2019) document significant impulse responses (IRs) of bond yields to shocks to news-based measures of policy uncertainty. While uncertainty and disagreement are fundamentally distinct concepts, one might reasonably connect a lack of clarity about government policies to the extent of disagreement in bond markets.
With this in mind, we focus on whether the predictive power ofHfor risk premiums is related to uncertainty about fiscal policy,PUfis, as constructed by Backer, Bloom, and Davis (2016) based on keywords in the media related to uncertainty about federal and local government spending. Substitution ofPUfisforHinBL's learning rule is not a viable strategy for quantifying this connection becausePUfisis far from a perfect proxy for the bond-relevant information embodied inH. Indeed, the full-sample projection ofPUfisonto contemporaneousHgives anR2less than37%.26Consequently, replacingHwithPUfis—learning withℓCG(P,PUfis)—leads to substantial deterioration inBL's forecast accuracy.
From the last row of TableVII, one can see that the learning ruleℓCG(P,[PUfis|H])performs as well asℓCG(P,H)across the maturity spectrum. This is a nontrivial finding. The fitted[PUfis|H]tfrom (22) is one-dimensional, so the comparable accuracy ofℓCG(P,[PUfis|H])requires that this specific linear combination ofHfully summarizes the predictive power of the two-dimensionalHfor bond risk premiums. The signs and magnitudes ofγ̂H,tfisare such that a positive shock to(ID(y2y)+ID(y10y))/2increases[PUfis|H]t. Increases in the level of yield disagreement are associated with increases in the component of fiscal policy uncertainty spanned byH, an intuitively plausible finding. To ensure that our findings are specific to fiscal policy uncertainty, we repeat this exercise after replacingPUfiswith each of the macroeconomic variables (M) introduced in the previous section and estimate the corresponding rulesℓCG(P,[M|H]). We report our results in AppendixE, where we show that none of these alternative rules is able to outperform ruleℓCG(P). This finding is consistent with the evidence discussed at the end of the previous section, which shows that the predictive content ofHis largely orthogonal to macroeconomic information.
Given this dimension reduction from conditioning on[PUfis|H]t, a central question that arises is whether shocks to[PUfis|H]timpact expected excess returns on bonds (risk premiums) in a comparable manner to shocks toH. To address this question, we compute the IRs ofP1andP2to one-standard-deviation shocks to[PUfis|H]t. Within the learning ruleℓCG(P,[PUfis|H]), the variables are ordered as[Pt′,[PUfis|H]t]. These IRs are compared to their “unrestricted” counterparts that arise in modelℓCG(P,H)by rotating thePautoregression for(Pt′,Ht′)to[Pt′,[PUfis|H]t,H1t⊥], where hereH1t⊥is the component ofH1that is orthogonal to[PUfis|H]t.27The shocks examined are for Januaries of 2000 (near the peak of the dot-com boom), 2009 (the outset of the global financial crisis), and 2014 (after a long period of falling rates). Figure11shows that the IRs across the two models are quite similar forP1, and even closer forP2. This result suggests that the constraints on the loadings onHfor excess returns implied by ruleℓCG(P,[PUfis|H])are largely consistent with theBL's “unrestricted” Bayesian learning ruleℓCG(P,H).
When estimating the learning rule using[EPU|H]([MPU|H]), the RMSE for excess returns on the 10-year bond increases to 6.23% (6.40%). Moreover, the IR correspondences weaken substantially, with those for ruleℓCG(P,[MPU|H])having completely opposite signs (directions) than those for ruleℓCG(P,H). The notable matching of IRs fromℓCG(P,[PUfis|H])andℓCG(P,H)stands out relative to other candidate measures of uncertainty.
article.VI. Discussion and Concluding Remarks:
Disagreement about the future evolution of yields, as measured by the cross-sectional dispersion of forecasts of 2- and 10-year bond yields by BCFF professionals (H), has substantial out-of-sample forecasting power for U.S. Treasury excess returns. Using a DTSM, our Bayesian learnerBLarrives at much more accurate out-of-sample forecasts when conditions onHthan when she conditions only on the yield PCs (P). The information inHimpacts her learning both through the updating of the parameters governing the dynamics ofPand as a direct predictor of future risk premiums. In contrast,BLtreats the parameters governing the risk-neutral distribution of the pricing factorsPas known and essentially constant over time.
BL's forecasts are more accurate relative to the consensus forecasts of BCFF professionals across the entire maturity spectrum and for both quarterly and annual forecast horizons. The outperformance of ruleℓCG(P,H)is especially large following recessions, when disagreement about the future two-year Treasury yield is relatively high and the professional consensus is that bond yields would rise much faster than they actually do. When comparingBL's learning rulesℓCG(P,H)andℓCG(P), the increased accuracy from conditioning onHis particularly evident beyond the five-year term to maturity.
The forecast power of investor disagreement does not arise from it serving as a stand-in for the risks of inflation and output growth. When we replaceHwith these business-cycle variables, there is a sizable decrease in forecast precision fromBL's learning rules. In contrast, we find a connection between uncertainty on future government spending and excess returns on Treasury bonds,PUtfis. Learning from theone-dimensionalprojection[PUfis|H]ofPUfisontoHtproduces nearly identical RMSEs to those from ruleℓCG(P,H). Moreover, the IRs of the yield PCs to fiscal policy shocks are quantitatively very similar across the learning modelsℓCG(P,H)andℓCG(P,[PUfis|H]).
Our findings do not imply that uncertainty about fiscal policy is a perfect proxy for the observed dispersion of yield forecasts by professionals. On the contrary,(Pt′,PUtfis)has limited explanatory power forHt. What we do show is that, after conditioning onPt, the component ofPUtfisthat is spanned byHthas roughly the same predictive power for future bond yields as the two-dimensionalHtdoes. Put differently, uncertainty about fiscal policy related to—channeled through?—investor disagreement has a material impact on the conditional distributions of the realized yields in U.S. Treasury bond markets. Our Bayesian econometrician exploits this insight to improve the accuracy of her learning rule relative toℓCG(P).
Finally, our learning model suggests that investor disagreement has a multidimensional relationship with risk premiums in bond markets: correlations between excess bond returns and disagreement about two-year bond yields have different signs and temporal patterns than the corresponding correlations for 10-year bond yields. The development of an equilibrium model that reconciles all of these connections among investor disagreement, policy uncertainties, and risk compensation in a learning setting represents an intriguing challenge for future research.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix C: Selecting the Constant-Gain:
Which value of the constant-gain coefficientγwould be selected by an econometrician using ruleℓCGL(P), presented in SectionIII, to forecast future yields in real time? To answer this question, we estimate the rule on an equally spaced grid of constant-gain parameters:{0.95,0.955,…,0.995,1}. We collect the one- and four-quarter-ahead forecasts for each value ofγand for each month from January 1982 through December 2014. In each month, we select the “optimal”γas the value that minimizes the root mean-squared error (RMSE) of out-of-sample forecasts for the first principal component (PC) over the previous 10 years. For example, in January 1982 we chooseγto minimize the out-of-sample RMSE between January 1971 (posttraining period) and January 1981.31FigureC1shows the evolution of the dynamically updatedγparameters (γt) over the sample from January 1982 through December 2014. For one-year-ahead forecasts, the constant-gain coefficient takes a value of 0.99 for most of the sample (Panel B), and especially in the period from the late 1990s to the end of the sample, which is the focus of most of the analysis in the paper. The evolution ofγtis more volatile when minimizing in real time the one-quarter-ahead RMSE of forecasts (Panel A), thoughγttakes values close to 0.99 in a large part of the sample period considered.
In FigureC2, we evaluate the ex post performance of different choices ofγ. The figure shows RMSEs, based on the benchmark learning ruleℓCGL(P), for the first PC of U.S. Treasury yields over the January 1995 through December 2014 sample period, and for different values ofγ. The minimal RMSE for one-year-ahead forecasts (Panel B) is achieved forγ=0.99. For the one-quarter-ahead forecasts (Panel A), the smallest RMSE is delivered by values ofγequal to 0.99 and 0.995.
Overall, the choice ofγ=0.99appears optimal (in the sense of minimizing forecast errors) or close to optimal both ex ante and ex post.
article.Appendix D: Bootstrapped D-M Statistics:
D-M statistics can be biased in small samples (see Diebold and Mariano (1995)). To address this issue, in TablesVandVIwe report adjusted D-M statistics based on the specification in Harvey, Leybourne, and Newbold (1997). To further corroborate that the D-M statistics reported in the main body of the paper are not distorted by small-sample bias, we also construct a bootstrapped version of the D-M test.
At the numerator, we useμ̂bootrather than the mean of the elements of vectordto account for potential bias in the sample mean ofdt. To ensure that the properties of the bootstrapped D-M statistic are not driven by the choice of block lengthl, we calculate the statistic in equation (D1) for three different choices of block length: 3 months, 12 months, and 24 months (which, when considering the full sample from January 1995 to December 2014, correspond to 80 blocks, 20 blocks, and 10 blocks).
TableDIreports the bootstrapped statistics for comparisons between the different yields forecasts reported in TableV, both at the quarterly and the annual predictive horizon, and for the sample from January 1995 to December 2014. The values of the bootstrapped statistics are robust to the choice of block length, and in line with those from the statistics based on Harvey, Leybourne, and Newbold (1997) that are reported in TableV.
In TableDII, we report the bootstrapped D-M statistics for one-year excess returns forecasts and for the subsamples considered in TableVI. In this case, due to the shorter length of the subsamples, we only report estimates based on block lengths of 3 and 12 months. The values of the statistics are more sensitive to the choice of block length than when considering the full sample, but results based on 12-months blocks are similar to those reported in the main body of the paper.
article.Appendix G: Robustness to Time-Varying V:
A less constrained Bayesian (relative toBL) would formally build updating ofΣPPinto her learning rule. A priori, we would not expect this generalization of our learning rules to materially affectBL's conditional forecasts of bond yields, our primary focus for modeling risk premiums. Updating ofΣPPwould only change the posterior conditional means indirectly through interactions withΘQ, passed onto theP-feedback parameters by the restrictions on the MPR. In our current setting,BLkeeps theQparameters(k∞Q,λQ)nearly constant. Therefore, it seems unlikely that formally introducing learning aboutΣPPwould lead to large changes in the inferred posterior conditionalP-means of bond yields.
FigureG1plots the eigenvalues of the feedback matricesK1PQandK1P,tPfrom the perspective ofBL's real-time learning rule in the presence ofVtand conditioning only on the history of the PCs. The eigenvalues ofK1PQare(ρQ,λQ)and the eigenvalues ofK1P,tPare(ρP,eig(KXtP)).32Relaxing the assumption of constant conditional volatility does not alter our prior finding that theQeigenvalues are nearly constant over the entire sample period. The variation in the eigenvalues ofK1P,tPreflects the substantial variation in the MPR.
FigureG2offers an interesting perspective on the degree to which the learning ruleℓCGL(P)(which presumes constantΣPP) captures the swings in the conditional covariance matrix that would be perceived by an agent learning in the presence of stochastic volatility. On the diagonal are the estimated conditional standard deviations from models both with and without stochastic volatility. RuleℓCGL(P)captures the overall evolution of the conditional standard deviations, but fails to pick up the huge increment in volatilities during the Fed experiment. Perceptions about volatility underℓCGL(P)also decay relatively slowly during the great moderation. The constant conditional correlations are updated byℓCGL(P)in a manner very similar to the learning rule for the stochastic volatility model.
